blocks.text: Before we write real programs, you need two tools: a way to save information, and a way to see it. That's it. Everything in JavaScript is built on top of those two things.

blocks.desc: Create a box called 'count' and put the number 1 inside it. The word 'let' means you're making a new box for the first time.
Look inside the box called 'count' and print whatever is in there. This is how you prove your code is working.

blocks.text: Here's a simple example of how to use variables and console.log:

lines: // 1. End every line with a semicolon  ;
// 2. Use 'let' the FIRST time you create a variable — never again after that

blocks.label: Cove says:
blocks.text: console.log is your best friend. Professional developers use it every single day. When something breaks, the first thing you do is console.log everything to figure out what's wrong.

challenges.title: Build your player card
challenges.description: Create three variables for a player card: their name, their starting score, and their favorite colour. Log all three. Then change the score to 99 and log it again.
challenges.hint: Log each variable with console.log. To change score, write score = 99 — no 'let' this time.
challenges.explanation: Variables are declared once with 'let'. To update a value later, just write the name without 'let' — otherwise JavaScript thinks you're making a new variable. console.log prints whatever is inside the variable at that moment, so logging before and after the change shows both values.

challenges.title: The scoreboard
challenges.description: A scoreboard needs to track a player's score as it changes. Start at 0, log it. Update to 50, log it. Update to 120, log it. Each log should show the current value at that moment.
challenges.hint: Use console.log(score) right after each change. To update score, write score = 50 — no 'let'.
challenges.explanation: You only use 'let' once — when the variable is first created. Every update after that is just score = newValue. console.log reads whatever is in the variable at that exact line, so the order matters: log before you change and you get the old value, log after and you get the new one.

exercises.title: Make your first variables
tasks: Declare a variable called age and set it to your age.
Declare a variable called pets and set it to how many pets you have.
Declare a variable called books and set it to how many books you own.
Run the code. Nothing will print yet — that comes next.
exercises.hint: Every variable starts with the word 'let'. Don't forget the semicolon at the end of each line.

exercises.title: Print your variables
tasks: Under each variable, add a console.log that prints it with a label.
Do the same for pets and books.
Run the code — you should see all 3 values in the console.
exercises.hint: The label goes inside quotes. Then a comma. Then the variable name — no quotes on the variable name.

exercises.title: Change a variable's value
tasks: Declare a variable called score and set it to 10.
Log score. Then on the next line, change score to 99 — no 'let' this time.
Log score again. You should see two different numbers printed.
Why no 'let' the second time? Discuss.
exercises.hint: You only use 'let' when creating a variable for the first time. After that, just write the name.
